behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at a module boundary (HTTP handler -> signature verifier -> queue writer). A pure Unit test can prove HMAC comparison logic, but it cannot catch a real wiring bug where the handler still forwards after a failed check. REAL-INFRA/LLM/MULTI-STEP do not apply. Rejection-gate: System is disqualified because behavior text only requires status code and no-forwarding, which can be reproduced in-process without real network/OS.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler in-process with a deterministic invalid signature and a spy/fake queue implementation. Assert HTTP 401 and assert queue write was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is ordering across modules (handler + persistent queue adapter). Unit is too low because the bug appears in handoff/order, not local logic. System is not required if persistence is exercised with local in-process test infrastructure (e.g., embedded/local DB or filesystem-backed queue object). No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Instrument queue write completion and response send in an integration test. Send a valid event and assert write commit timestamp/flag occurs before HTTP 200 is observed.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Behavior verifies decoupling between enqueue path and downstream worker path, which is cross-module orchestration. Unit is too low because waiting/non-waiting often fails at async boundary wiring. System is disqualified: no requirement for real network/OS behavior, only async separation.
behaviors.test_description: Use an integration harness where downstream processor intentionally blocks (e.g., promise resolved later). Assert HTTP response returns before processor completion, while enqueue succeeds.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time concurrency SLA. Event-loop contention, socket handling, scheduler effects, and concurrent request pressure are real runtime phenomena that mocks can hide. Requires real wall-clock measurement under concurrent load; therefore System.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real service process, generate concurrent slow downstream workload from earlier events, then send new valid webhook requests over real HTTP and assert response latency <3s at high-percentile/strict bound.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup is a state-handoff/persistence interaction (handler + dedup store/queue uniqueness), not just local function logic. Unit can miss race/state bugs. System is disqualified because this can be caught with local integration infrastructure (same process, local persistent store).
behaviors.test_description: Submit the same event ID twice through the handler in an integration test backed by the real dedup/persistence layer used in-process. Assert only one queue record exists/write succeeds once.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is translation of a queue backpressure signal into HTTP semantics across module boundaries. Unit is too low because a real failure is often incorrect wiring/mapping of queue error -> HTTP response/header. System is disqualified by behavior text: it only requires handling the 'queue reports full' condition, which is reproducible in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler to a queue adapter stub/fake that deterministically returns 'full'. Assert HTTP 503 and presence/value format of Retry-After header.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This tests the real queue component with its persistence backing, not a mocked contract. Unit is too low because capacity enforcement depends on interaction with storage/state across writes. System was considered, but disqualified because behavior does not require external network/subprocess/OS primitives beyond local persistence wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize queue with a small configured capacity using its real local persistence backend, enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more and assert deterministic rejection/error without data corruption.
